content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTCB">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA0</dxn>

</object>

British Airways"
      }

  "cursor": "1"

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTX4" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTX4" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper) <!-- id=task_1 -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk <!-- id=task_2 -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan and melt butter <!-- id=task_3 -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Pour whisked eggs into the pan <!-- id=task_4 -->
  - **TODO**: Stir and scramble eggs until cooked <!-- id=task_5 -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt and pepper, serve on a plate <!-- id=task_6 -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTXZ">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTX4</dxn>

</object>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

19. Eggs formed soft, fluffy curds throughout the pan.
20. Turned off stove burner (residual heat will finish cooking).

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTX4" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper) <!-- id=task_1 -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk <!-- id=task_2 -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan and melt butter <!-- id=task_3 -->
  - **DONE**: Pour whisked eggs into the pan <!-- id=task_4 -->
  - **DONE**: Stir and scramble eggs until cooked <!-- id=task_5 -->
  - **DONE**: Season with salt and pepper, serve on a plate <!-- id=task_6 -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTXZ">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTX4</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>
Go

Let me start by creating the tasks and the initial markdown document.

params.content:
# State

- 2 scrambled eggs (in frying pan, soft curds, fully cooked)
- 2 empty eggshells (in trash)
- 1 frying pan (on stove, contains cooked scrambled eggs)
- 1 bowl (on counter, empty)
- 1 whisk (on counter, used)
- 1 spatula (on counter, used)
- 1 plate (on counter)
- Salt (on counter)
- Pepper (on counter)
- Stove burner (off)

# Action Log

1. Took 2 raw eggs out of the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Got frying pan from shelf and placed on counter.
4. Got bowl from cupboard and placed on counter.
5. Got whisk from drawer and placed on counter.
6. Got plate from cupboard and placed on counter.
7. Got salt and pepper from pantry and placed on counter.
8. Cracked egg #1 into the bowl, discarded shell.
9. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
10. Whisked eggs in bowl until well combined and slightly frothy.
11. Placed frying pan on stove burner.
12. Turned stove burner on to medium heat.
13. Added knob of butter to the warming pan.
14. Butter melted and coated the bottom of the pan evenly.
15. Poured whisked eggs from bowl into the hot buttered pan.
16. Eggs began to sizzle and set at the edges.
17. Used spatula to gently push eggs from edges toward center, forming soft curds.
18. Continued stirring and folding eggs for ~2 minutes.
19. Eggs formed soft, fluffy curds throughout the pan.
20. Turned off stove burner (residual heat will finish cooking).

params.content:
# State

- 1 plate of scrambled eggs (seasoned with salt and pepper, ready to eat!) 🍳
- 2 empty eggshells (in trash)
- 1 frying pan (on stove, empty, cooling)
- 1 bowl (on counter, dirty)
- 1 whisk (on counter, dirty)
- 1 spatula (on counter, dirty)
- Salt (on counter)
- Pepper (on counter)
- Stove burner (off)

# Action Log

1. Took 2 raw eggs out of the fridge and placed on counter.
2. Took butter out of the fridge and placed on counter.
3. Got frying pan from shelf and placed on counter.
4. Got bowl from cupboard and placed on counter.
5. Got whisk from drawer and placed on counter.
6. Got plate from cupboard and placed on counter.
7. Got salt and pepper from pantry and placed on counter.
8. Cracked egg #1 into the bowl, discarded shell.
9. Cracked egg #2 into the bowl, discarded shell.
10. Whisked eggs in bowl until well combined and slightly frothy.
11. Placed frying pan on stove burner.
12. Turned stove burner on to medium heat.
13. Added knob of butter to the warming pan.
14. Butter melted and coated the bottom of the pan evenly.
15. Poured whisked eggs from bowl into the hot buttered pan.
16. Eggs began to sizzle and set at the edges.
17. Used spatula to gently push eggs from edges toward center, forming soft curds.
18. Continued stirring and folding eggs for ~2 minutes.
19. Eggs formed soft, fluffy curds throughout the pan.
20. Turned off stove burner (residual heat will finish cooking).
21. Slid scrambled eggs from pan onto the plate.
22. Sprinkled salt over the eggs.
23. Sprinkled pepper over the eggs.
24. Scrambled eggs are ready to serve! Bon appétit! 🎉

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFG">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6</dxn>

</object>

Dear Mr. Smith,

FLIGHT DETAILS

Flight: BA287

Arrival: 12:15 GMT (+1 day)

PASSENGER
─────────────────────────────────────
John Smith

PAYMENT SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────
Base fare:              £485.00

─────────────────────────────────────
TOTAL PAID:             £612.50

Payment method: Visa ending in 4521
Transaction date: 28 January 2026

Please keep this email for your records.

Kind regards,
British Airways"
      }
    ]
  },
  "cursor": "1"
}
</event>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFG">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFG">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

</object>

tools.name: add-artifact
tools.description: Adds a new artifact.

name.description: The name of the artifact to add.

artifact.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFG">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6" name="Expense tracking">
    <spec>
      Keep a list of expenses in a markdown document (create artifact "Expenses").
  Process incoming emails, add the relevant ones to the list.
  
  Format:
  
  ## Expenses
  - Flight to London (2026-02-01): £100
  - Hotel in London (2026-02-01): £100
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTFG">
        Expenses
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD6</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTZD" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

</object>

tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

tools.name: update-tasks
tools.description:
TASK MANAGEMENT TOOL - USAGE GUIDELINES

This tool maintains an organized task list during work sessions to track progress, break down objectives, and ensure thoroughness. After creating initial tasks, update them silently without announcing changes to the user.

=== CORE USAGE PRINCIPLES ===

Create and manage tasks for: multi-step objectives requiring 3+ distinct actions, complex projects needing careful sequencing, user requests for task organization, multiple deliverables provided together, new instructions (capture as tasks immediately with new IDs), completed work (mark done and add follow-ups), and active work (mark as 'in-progress', limit one at a time).

Skip task management for: single straightforward actions, simple requests achievable in 1-2 steps, informational queries, quick lookups or clarifications, and avoid creating verification tasks unless requested.

=== TOOL SPECIFICATION ===

update-tasks requires an array of task objects. Each task object contains:
- id (string, required): unique identifier like "task_1" or "research_sources"
- title (string, optional): update to clarify or refine task description
- status (string, optional): 'todo' | 'in-progress' | 'done'

Task status meanings: 'todo' means not yet started, 'in-progress' means currently being worked on, 'done' means completed successfully.

=== OPERATIONAL GUIDELINES ===

Update tasks in real-time as work progresses. Mark tasks 'done' immediately upon completion. Maintain only ONE task with 'in-progress' status at a time. Complete current tasks before starting new ones. Use specific, actionable task titles. Break complex work into manageable logical pieces. Batch task updates with other actions when possible for efficiency.

=== USAGE EXAMPLES ===

<example type="research_project">
<user_message>I need to research sustainable packaging options and write a report comparing costs and environmental impact.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "research_materials", title: "Research sustainable packaging materials", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "compare_costs", title: "Compile cost comparison data", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "assess_impact", title: "Evaluate environmental impact metrics", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "draft_report", title: "Write comparative analysis report", status: "todo"}

Begins research work immediately in same response.
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multi-phase project requiring systematic tracking across research, analysis, and writing stages.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="event_planning">
<user_message>Help me plan a team retreat - venue, agenda, catering, and send invitations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "venue_options", title: "Research and recommend venues", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "draft_agenda", title: "Create retreat agenda", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "arrange_catering", title: "Coordinate catering options", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "send_invites", title: "Draft and send invitations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Multiple distinct deliverables with dependencies requiring organized tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="document_analysis">
<user_message>Review this 30-page contract for key terms, risks, and negotiation points.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "extract_key_terms", title: "Identify key contractual terms", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "flag_risks", title: "Flag potential risks and liabilities", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "negotiation_points", title: "Compile negotiation recommendations", status: "todo"}
4. {id: "summary_doc", title: "Prepare executive summary", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Substantial analysis work requiring methodical breakdown and systematic review.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="multiple_deliverables">
<user_message>I need: 1) competitor analysis for three companies, 2) SWOT analysis, 3) market positioning recommendations.</user_message>
<assistant_action>
Creates tasks:
1. {id: "competitor_research", title: "Research three competitor companies", status: "in-progress"}
2. {id: "swot_analysis", title: "Develop SWOT analysis", status: "todo"}
3. {id: "positioning_recs", title: "Create market positioning recommendations", status: "todo"}
</assistant_action>
<reasoning>User provided numbered list of distinct deliverables requiring separate effort.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_simple_question">
<user_message>What's the difference between renewable and sustainable energy?</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides explanation directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Informational request with no actionable work to complete or track.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_quick_lookup">
<user_message>Find the population of Tokyo.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Searches and provides answer without task tracking.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single straightforward lookup completable immediately.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_trivial_task">
<user_message>Summarize this 2-paragraph email.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Provides summary directly without creating tasks.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>Single simple action requiring no breakdown or progress tracking.</reasoning>
</example>

<example type="skip_single_action">
<user_message>Check if my flight is on time.</user_message>
<assistant_action>Performs lookup and reports status without task management.</assistant_action>
<reasoning>One-step action with immediate completion, no organizational benefit from tasks.</reasoning>
</example>

=== BEST PRACTICES ===

For task creation: use descriptive unique IDs reflecting the work, start first task as 'in-progress', batch initial creation with beginning work. For progress tracking: update status immediately upon completion, keep only one 'in-progress' task unless parallel work is natural, add follow-up tasks as they emerge. For task breakdown: aim for reasonably-scoped tasks, group related small actions into logical units, split tasks requiring different approaches.

When uncertain whether to use task management, err on the side of creating tasks. Proactive organization demonstrates thoroughness and ensures comprehensive work completion.

content.content:
You are a helpful assistant that can help with tasks in the outside world.
Be transparent about what you are doing and what you are not doing.
If you do not have tools to complete the task, inform the user.
DO NOT PRETEND TO DO SOMETHING YOU CAN'T DO.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You work on an project. Each project has a spec - the goal of the project.
  The project plan shows the current progress of the project.
  Project has an number of associated artifacts you can read/write.
  Spec and plan are also artifacts.
  You can edit them if necessary.
  
  IMPORTANT: When create a new artifact, always add it to the project using the add-artifact function.
  
  <project id="01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTZD" name="Egg making">
    <spec>
      I'm testing how planning (task management) works.
  Create tasks to make scrambled eggs.
  
  Then simulate this plan execution in a markdown document.
  The document should reflect the state of all objects involved in the cooking process.
  The document should also have the log of actions taken.
  
  Important: simualte actions one by one, in the order they are listed.
  Simlate by updating the local document.
  
  <example>
    # State
  
    - 2 raw eggs
    - 1 frying pan
    
    # Action log
    
    - Taken 2 raw eggs out of the fridge.
  </example>
    </spec>
    <plan>
      - **DONE**: Gather ingredients (eggs, butter, salt, pepper) <!-- id=task_1 -->
  - **DONE**: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk <!-- id=task_2 -->
  - **DONE**: Heat frying pan and melt butter <!-- id=task_3 -->
  - **IN-PROGRESS**: Pour whisked eggs into the pan <!-- id=task_4 -->
  - **TODO**: Stir and scramble eggs until cooked <!-- id=task_5 -->
  - **TODO**: Season with salt and pepper <!-- id=task_6 -->
  - **TODO**: Plate and serve <!-- id=task_7 -->
    </plan>
  
    <artifacts>
      <artifact type="org.dxos.type.document" dxn="dxn:echo:@:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNV60">
        Scrambled Eggs Simulation
      </artifact>
    </artifacts>
  </project>
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTZD</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.project</typename>
</object>
Go

content.text:
The user wants me to execute the project plan for making scrambled eggs. Let me break this down:

1. First, create tasks for making scrambled eggs
2. Create a markdown document to simulate the plan execution
3. Simulate actions one by one, updating the document

Let me start by creating the tasks and the initial markdown document.
Let's get started! I'll first create the task plan for making scrambled eggs, then simulate execution step by step in a markdown document.
